rationale_for_baseline_tier: An 8-year-old with a feed-dependent hepatic metabolic disorder has had a hypoglycaemic seizure with a documented home glucose of 2.1 mmol/L and remains confused with compensatory tachypnoea. This is an immediate threat to life: profound neuroglycopenia risks recurrent seizures, coma and irreversible brain injury, and there is a probable lactic acidosis. A remote assessor cannot secure the airway if he seizes again, cannot measure serial glucose or blood gases, and cannot confirm neurological recovery — so despite the in-person clinician stabilising him at home, the telehealth-correct action is T5 (call 000) while coaching immediate rescue carbohydrate. Emergency services provide airway protection, IV glucose, acidosis correction and observation for recurrence.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Another seizure, or he cannot be fully woken / becomes more drowsy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately and keep him on his side; do not put anything in his mouth during a seizure
escalation_edges.rationale: A recurrent seizure or falling consciousness signals ongoing severe neuroglycopenia and an airway at risk — paramedic resuscitation and IV glucose are the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has another shaking episode or you can't wake him properly, call 000 straight away, lay him on his side, and don't put anything in his mouth — stay on the line with the ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blood sugar stays low or drops below 3.5 mmol/L again despite the glucose and cornstarch
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If it does not come up and stay up quickly, he needs emergency IV glucose — 000 now
escalation_edges.rationale: Persisting or recurring hypoglycaemia in a feed-dependent GSD child means enteral carbohydrate is not holding and IV glucose is required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Keep checking his blood sugar; if it stays low or drops below 3.5 again even after the sugar and cornstarch, that's an emergency — call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Breathing becomes very fast and deep (worsening)
escalation_edges.rationale: Worsening deep, fast breathing indicates a worsening metabolic (lactic) acidosis that needs hospital assessment and correction.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his breathing gets very fast and deep, that's a sign the acid in his blood is building up — he needs to be seen urgently, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He vomits the cornstarch / cannot keep the rescue carbohydrate down
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If already awaiting an ambulance, tell them; otherwise go straight to the Emergency Department
escalation_edges.rationale: If he cannot retain enteral carbohydrate he cannot self-correct and needs IV glucose in hospital.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he brings the cornstarch back up and can't keep sugar down, he needs to be in hospital for a sugar drip — go to the Emergency Department or tell the ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: 8-year-old with an inborn error of metabolism and essentially no fasting tolerance
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate and never withhold glucose to obtain tests; doses come from his own emergency plan and the metabolic team, never a remote paediatric calculation.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Both parents present but highly distressed after witnessing the seizure
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give short, clear, stepwise instructions (sugar now, call 000, keep him safe on his side), confirm they have called 000, and stay with them until help arrives.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them he has a metabolic condition and has had a low-sugar seizure. If he is awake enough to swallow safely, give his fast-acting glucose straight away and then reconnect his feed / give his usual cornstarch. If he is too drowsy or is shaking, lay him on his side, keep him safe, and don't put anything in his mouth. Keep checking his blood sugar and stay with him until the ambulance arrives.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or the planned telehealth review.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hospital to provide a discharge summary; share the crisis and the feed-disconnection cause with the paediatric metabolic team and GP, and review the overnight-feeding safety setup (locking connectors, dual alarm).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a post-seizure, confused, profoundly hypoglycaemic feed-dependent child as a routine or same-day problem rather than an emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Recurrent hypoglycaemic seizure with unprotected airway, coma and irreversible brain injury from delayed emergency care.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing self-transport to ED rather than an ambulance for a child who may seize again in transit and needs airway protection and IV glucose.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is appropriate here; there is no meaningful over-triage risk for an active hypoglycaemic seizure emergency.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: A remote assessor cannot confirm sustained neurological recovery, verify ongoing glucose stability, or measure the degree of acidosis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to 000, coach immediate rescue carbohydrate, and keep the carer monitoring and reporting; treat transient improvement as insufficient evidence of safety.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Being reassured by a brief improvement after glucose and standing the family down to a GP appointment or the planned 4-hour telehealth review.
